health_predispositions.description: Joint malformation causing pain and mobility issues, common in large breeds
One of the highest cancer rates of any breed, particularly after age 6

behavioral_traits.temperament: Friendly, devoted, eager to please. One of the most food-motivated breeds.

behavioral_traits.anxiety_profile: Generally low anxiety but can develop separation anxiety. Sensitive to owner emotions.

Mouthy breed — likes to carry objects, increasing foreign body ingestion risk
Loves water — increased ear infection risk after swimming

age_specific_concerns.puppy_0_to_1: Joint development critical — avoid high-impact exercise. Monitor growth rate.
age_specific_concerns.young_adult_1_to_3: High energy period. Establish exercise routine. Watch for early allergy signs.
age_specific_concerns.adult_3_to_7: Maintain healthy weight. Annual vet checkups. Monitor for early joint stiffness.
age_specific_concerns.senior_7_plus: Cancer screening becomes important. Joint supplements may help. Watch for cognitive changes, exercise intolerance, unexplained lumps.

common_medications.notes: Common in senior Goldens. Monitor for GI side effects.

embedding_text: Golden Retriever. Large sporting breed, 55-75 lbs, life expectancy 10-12 years, senior at 8. Extremely food-motivated — skipping meals is highly unusual. Prone to: hip/elbow dysplasia, cancer (hemangiosarcoma, lymphoma, osteosarcoma — one of highest cancer rates), heart disease, ear infections (floppy ears, worse after swimming), skin allergies, obesity. Mouthy breed with foreign body ingestion risk. Loves water. Common meds: NSAIDs for joints, Apoquel for allergies, joint supplements. Red flags: sudden collapse, new lumps, meal skipping, persistent ear issues.

Floppy ears trap moisture, especially after swimming

Active breed prone to knee ligament injuries, especially if overweight

embedding_text: Labrador Retriever. Large sporting breed, 55-80 lbs, life expectancy 10-12 years, senior at 8. Most food-motivated breed due to POMC gene — refusal to eat is a major warning sign. Prone to: hip/elbow dysplasia, obesity (genetic predisposition), ear infections (floppy ears, worse after swimming), exercise-induced collapse (EIC), cruciate ligament tears. Highest risk breed for foreign body ingestion and poisoning. Common meds: NSAIDs for joints, Apoquel for allergies. Red flags: food refusal, exercise collapse, sudden hind lameness, ingestion of toxins.

embedding_text: German Shepherd. Large herding breed, 50-90 lbs, life expectancy 9-13 years, senior at 8. Notoriously sensitive stomach. Prone to: hip/elbow dysplasia, degenerative myelopathy (progressive hind weakness), EPI (most predisposed breed — weight loss despite eating), IBD, bloat/GDV. High pain tolerance masks discomfort. Stress manifests as GI symptoms. Common meds: pancreatic enzymes for EPI, NSAIDs, probiotics. Red flags: progressive hind weakness, weight loss with good appetite, bloat symptoms, chronic diarrhea.

embedding_text: French Bulldog. Small brachycephalic breed, 16-28 lbs, life expectancy 10-12 years, senior at 9. Compressed airway anatomy — breathing issues are primary concern. Prone to: brachycephalic airway syndrome, IVDD, skin fold infections, allergies (one of most allergy-prone breeds), heat intolerance. Lower baseline energy — some lethargy is normal. Heat sensitive. Common meds: Apoquel for allergies, medicated wipes, gabapentin for nerve pain. Red flags: breathing difficulty beyond normal, blue gums, yelping/reluctance to move (IVDD), collapse in heat.

embedding_text: Dachshund. Small hound breed, 11-32 lbs, life expectancy 12-16 years, senior at 10. Elongated spine and short legs make IVDD the #1 concern — highest IVDD rate of any breed. Prone to: IVDD (any reluctance to jump is a warning), obesity (extra weight worsens spinal stress), dental disease, patellar luxation. Every jump stresses the spine. Ramps essential. Back pain may show as aggression. Common meds: gabapentin, muscle relaxants, NSAIDs. Red flags: ANY jump reluctance, dragging hind legs (emergency), yelping when touched, sudden paralysis.

embedding_text: Border Collie. Medium herding breed, 30-55 lbs, life expectancy 12-15 years, senior at 10. Most intelligent dog breed — extremely high mental stimulation needs. Prone to: hip dysplasia, epilepsy (higher incidence, onset 1-5 years), collie eye anomaly, compulsive behaviors (from understimulation). Fixation on walls/lights may be compulsive behavior OR real stimulus detection. Extremely sensitive to routine changes. Common meds: anti-epileptics, fluoxetine for compulsive behaviors. Red flags: seizures, uninterruptible fixation, vision problems, dramatic behavioral change.

embedding_text: Rottweiler. Large working breed, 80-135 lbs, life expectancy 8-10 years, senior at 7. Significant cardiac and cancer risks. Prone to: DCM (coughing at night is key warning), subaortic stenosis, hip/elbow dysplasia, osteosarcoma (persistent single-limb limping), cruciate tears. Stoic — hides pain. Aggression may indicate pain. Common meds: pimobendan and ACE inhibitors for heart, NSAIDs. Red flags: nighttime coughing, exercise intolerance/fainting, persistent single-limb limping, sudden collapse.

Common meds: pimobendan for heart, joint supplements. Red flags: ANY abdominal distension/retching (bloat), coughing (DCM), persistent single-limb limping (osteosarcoma).

embedding_text: Chihuahua. Toy breed, 2-6 lbs, life expectancy 14-16 years, senior at 12. Tiny size means minor illnesses escalate rapidly. Prone to: patellar luxation, hypoglycemia (especially puppies — not eating 12+ hours is emergency), heart disease (mitral valve), dental disease (almost universal), collapsed trachea. Shaking can be normal but persistent trembling may indicate pain or low blood sugar. Open fontanelle makes head trauma dangerous. Common meds: heart medications, cough suppressants, emergency corn syrup. Red flags: not eating 12+ hours, wobbliness/glazed eyes (hypoglycemia), honking cough, head trauma.

breed_specific_red_flags: Any new lump or skin mass (mast cell tumor risk — very high in Boxers)

embedding_text: Boxer. Large working breed, 50-80 lbs, life expectancy 10-12 years, senior at 8. One of highest cancer rates — mast cell tumors especially common. Prone to: cancer (mast cell tumors, lymphoma, brain tumors), Boxer cardiomyopathy (breed-specific arrhythmia), bloat/GDV, hip dysplasia, GI sensitivity. High pain tolerance masks illness. Fast eater increases bloat risk. Common meds: cardiac anti-arrhythmics, slow feeders. Red flags: new lumps, fainting/collapse, bloat symptoms, seizures in older Boxers.

embedding_text: Pomeranian. Toy breed, 3-7 lbs, life expectancy 12-16 years, senior at 11. High cardiac risk — heart disease is leading cause of death. Prone to: mitral valve disease, collapsed trachea, patellar luxation, hypoglycemia, Alopecia X. Tiny size means falls are dangerous. Shaking can be normal but persistent trembling needs evaluation. Common meds: heart medications, cough suppressants, emergency corn syrup. Red flags: coughing/exercise intolerance (heart), honking cough (trachea), wobbliness (hypoglycemia), collapse.

embedding_text: Shih Tzu. Small toy breed, 9-16 lbs, life expectancy 10-16 years, senior at 11. Brachycephalic with prominent vulnerable eyes. Prone to: brachycephalic airway issues, eye problems (proptosis, dry eye, corneal ulcers), Cushing's disease (one of most predisposed — excessive drinking is key sign), dental disease, ear infections. Excessive drinking is NOT normal. Heat sensitive. Common meds: trilostane for Cushing's, eye drops, allergy medications. Red flags: eye injury/bulging, excessive drinking (Cushing's), breathing difficulty, eye trauma.

embedding_text: Yorkshire Terrier. Toy breed, 4-7 lbs, life expectancy 11-15 years, senior at 11. One of worst breeds for dental disease. Prone to: dental disease (severe, starts young), collapsed trachea, portosystemic liver shunt (disorientation/seizures after eating), patellar luxation, hypoglycemia. Disorientation after meals is a critical liver shunt warning. Common meds: lactulose for liver, cough suppressants, dental care. Red flags: disorientation/seizures after eating, honking cough, not eating 12+ hours, facial swelling.

health_predispositions.description: One of the most allergy-prone breeds — skin and GI manifestations
Moderate risk in this muscular active breed

behavioral_traits.temperament: Loyal, affectionate, eager to please. Strong and athletic.

Strong jaw and chewing drive — foreign body ingestion risk

High pain tolerance means limping is always significant

common_medications.notes: Very commonly needed. May need lifelong management.

embedding_text: Pit Bull / Pit Bull Mix. Medium terrier, 30-65 lbs, life expectancy 12-14 years, senior at 9. One of most allergy-prone breeds. Prone to: skin/food allergies (dominant concern), hip dysplasia, cruciate ligament tears, thyroid disease, demodectic mange. Extremely stoic — hides pain, so limping is always significant. Allergies cause both skin and GI symptoms. Common meds: Apoquel/Cytopoint, limited ingredient diets, NSAIDs. Red flags: limping (stoic breed), chronic skin issues, sudden hind lameness, behavioral change.

embedding_text: English Bulldog. Medium breed, 40-50 lbs, life expectancy 8-10 years, senior at 7. Most severely brachycephalic breed — breathing is the defining health concern. Prone to: severe brachycephalic airway syndrome, skin fold infections, hip dysplasia, cherry eye, heat stroke (most heat-sensitive breed). Cannot exercise in warm weather. Cannot swim. Loud breathing is baseline normal. Common meds: skin care products, NSAIDs, allergy meds. Red flags: breathing difficulty beyond normal, blue gums, heat collapse, eye problems.

embedding_text: Cocker Spaniel. Medium sporting breed, 20-30 lbs, life expectancy 12-14 years, senior at 10. THE breed most prone to chronic ear infections. Prone to: chronic ear infections, multiple eye diseases, AIHA (autoimmune — pale gums/yellow eyes is EMERGENCY), hypothyroidism, seborrhea. Happy temperament — behavioral changes very noticeable. Common meds: ear cleaners weekly, immunosuppressives for AIHA, eye medications. Red flags: pale gums/yellow eyes (AIHA emergency), dark urine, eye pain, chronic ear infections.

embedding_text: Cavalier King Charles Spaniel. Small toy breed, 12-18 lbs, life expectancy 9-14 years, senior at 9. Nearly 100% develop mitral valve disease — heart disease is THE defining issue. Prone to: MVD (murmur by age 5 typical), syringomyelia (skull too small for brain — phantom scratching is hallmark), eye conditions, patellar luxation. Phantom scratching is NOT a skin issue — it's neurological. Very separation-anxious. Common meds: cardiac medications, gabapentin for SM pain, eye drops. Red flags: phantom scratching, coughing/rapid breathing (heart), fainting, yelping when lowering head.

embedding_text: Miniature Schnauzer. Small terrier, 11-20 lbs, life expectancy 12-15 years, senior at 10. THE breed most predisposed to pancreatitis — high-fat foods are extremely dangerous. Prone to: pancreatitis (breed #1 risk), hyperlipidemia, bladder stones, diabetes, cataracts. High-fat treats can trigger life-threatening pancreatitis. Excessive drinking is always concerning. Common meds: low-fat diet (critical), insulin if diabetic, urinary diet. Red flags: vomiting with abdominal pain (pancreatitis), excessive drinking (diabetes), blood in urine (stones).

embedding_text: Doberman Pinscher. Large working breed, 60-100 lbs, life expectancy 10-12 years, senior at 8. THE breed most affected by DCM — up to 58% develop it, can cause sudden death. Prone to: DCM (annual Holter monitoring critical), von Willebrand's disease (excessive bleeding), bloat/GDV, Wobbler syndrome, hip dysplasia. Annual cardiac screening is non-negotiable. Common meds: cardiac medications (often pre-symptom), DDAVP for clotting. Red flags: coughing/exercise intolerance (DCM), fainting/collapse, excessive bleeding, bloat symptoms.

embedding_text: Goldendoodle. Mixed/designer breed, 30-75 lbs, life expectancy 10-15 years, senior at 9. Inherits risks from Golden Retriever (cancer, hip dysplasia) and Poodle (Addison's, epilepsy, eye diseases). Prone to: hip dysplasia, allergies, chronic ear infections, Addison's disease, cancer. Variable size affects risk profile. Non-shedding coat hides skin issues. Common meds: allergy medications, ear cleaners, joint supplements. Red flags: intermittent vomiting/lethargy (Addison's), new lumps (cancer), chronic ear infections.

embedding_text: Pembroke Welsh Corgi. Small herding breed, 25-30 lbs, life expectancy 12-13 years, senior at 10. Long back + short legs = IVDD risk similar to Dachshunds. One of most predisposed breeds to DM (progressive hind weakness). Prone to: IVDD, DM, hip dysplasia, obesity (weight management critical for spine), von Willebrand's. Progressive hind weakness is NOT just aging. Common meds: NSAIDs, weight management diet. Red flags: progressive hind weakness (DM), back pain (IVDD), rapid weight gain, dragging feet.
